text: to master his human imperfections, his pride and his self-love. Hobbes’ solution was to tame the passions with the Leviathan, agreed upon by fear-driven individuals. Only in this way would natural man become civilized, and only in this way would peace once again prevail. The Hobbesian Person If Hobbes’ philosophical anthropology seems overly simplified and openly functional to his following analysis, his reduction of the self must nonetheless be taken seriously, for after some initial strong criticism, the Hobbesian person did become foundational in political matters. The Hobbesian view of human nature 12 In his presentation of Hobbes, Voegelin makes the dubious (and undocumented) statement that the biblical symbol of the Leviathan had been designed by an English thinker in direct response to the Puritan danger (Voegelin 2000: 240). Martinich (1992: 362) follows the more generally-accepted suggestion that Hobbes designed the frontispiece himself. 13 Descartes criticized Hobbes’ work on precisely these grounds. Commenting on Hobbes’ De Cive of 1642, Descartes remarked that Hobbes’ could have achieved his goals better by adopting more ‘solid and virtuous principles’ than those upon which he in fact grounded his case (Descartes in Sommerville 1992: 3). To Descartes, Hobbes’ principles of human nature were ‘very wicked and very dangerous, in that he supposes that all men are evil, or that he gives them reason to be so’ (Descartes in ibid.: 41). Hobbes, conversely, saw nothing constructive in Descartes’ arguments for scepticism (Williams 1998: 48), as he had made clear in his objections to Descartes’ Meditations on First Philosophy in 1641. However, and as discussed by Tuck (2002), these mutual dismissals must also be understood as comments made by two jealous rivals competing in their geometrical search for the first principles of the new science. Liminality and the Modern 130 still exerts great influence on political theory today, most visibly within various schools of rational choice theory and the realist paradigm in international relations. It was connected to a larger world-view. John Ruggie claims that on the level of social metaphysics, modern territorial rulers from the seventeenth century started to view society as ‘a collection of atomistic and autonomous bodies-in-motion in a field of forces energized solely by scarcity and ambition’ (Ruggie 1993: 158). The autonomous state of Hobbes rested upon selfish passion-driven individuals, whose order rested in and upon the polity itself. The possibility of peacefully keeping together a society thus came to reside in the negative definition of the human person. While this is a very different reduction of the self when compared with the narrowing of being established in the Cartesian system, the effects of the two constructs can reinforce each other: the rational order that follows in both cases establishes a specifically empty and delimited understanding of the human personality – a view which in all other historical periods would have stood no chance of becoming accepted (see Szakolczai 2011). The construct Hobbes was after must cut off both ‘nature’ and ‘divinity’, personal belief and transcendental truth, in one stroke. Society should be held together, peace and order secured, by a civil theology whose indisputable truth resided within itself, which it itself represented, and which could not be brought to judgment by any external source of power but itself. Human nature and God’s order would then come together as one, both ordained by the Sovereign. 14 Reduced to a selfish animal, the Hobbesian person became authentically human only through the higher reality of the Sovereign. 15 The Sovereign, in his turn, represented pure order. He, symbolizing the state, was the ordered Cartesian self of politics from whom truth flows. Although in Leviathan Hobbes very explicitly discerned God’s role in society from that of the Sovereign, at a deeper level this means that the Sovereign comes to stand in the same kind of direct contact with God, such that truth and the order of society simply are God himself. 14 The minor weakness of this seemingly perfect system, of which Hobbes was pragmatically aware, was that the problem of passion-blind, power-thirsty individuals was duplicated at the inter-state level since each state was a self-enclosed, power-maximizing unit pitted against every other in a continuous state of war. War was from that perspective the normal and expected state of affairs, peace just a prelude to another war. Between and over states there was, and should not be, any supreme Sovereign. This being so, international peace accords would come to represent the temporary absence of war. In practice this principle became accepted. Much later it was built into modern political geography when Ratzel (1897) claimed that states would most naturally seek territorial expansion, and that only a fear of defeat would hold them back. The realist paradigm in international relations theory always took this Hobbesian point of view as an unquestioned premise. 15 Hobbes’ definition of the person goes with his theory of representation and runs as follows: ‘A person, is he, whose words or actions are considered, either as his own, or as representing the words and actions of another man, or of any other thing’. The natural person is no good to Hobbes. The good and civil person is he who ceases to exist as natural by fusing into the higher reality of the Leviathan. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 131 We should understand this construct well, for in Voegelin’s reading this is critically what underlies the gnostic nature of the Hobbesian state, and which renders meaning to the very term ‘absolute’. Importantly, Hobbes did not rule out religion or nature. In fact, as is often overlooked, a major part of Leviathan is dedicated to a purely theological discussion, and in this sense Hobbes writes well within a theological paradigm. Hobbes wanted to bring Christianity down to earth, and he does so by interpreting the Bible against Greek philosophy, which was charged with having ‘unduly spiritualized Christianity’ (Pacchi 2002: 185). To Hobbes, divine law and natural law were to function only insofar as they respected the social laws, the truth of society. In
